speaker: interviewer
text: So this tape number three with our interview with Jack Sandbrand, S-A-N-D-B-R-A-N-D. Today's date is January 22, 1997. We are at Rancho Bernardo, San Diego, California. Jack, you mentioned that your mother had a radio that she bought before 1939. When the Russians came in, was she able to keep her radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, having a radio, were you aware of what was going on in the war between 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that the German army took half of Poland and the Soviet army took the other half, which was the clear part of Western Ukraine, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We knew that the area north of us became part of Belarus. Bialystok and all that area. And we also knew when the-- a year later I believe when the Russians, the Soviet army, moved in on Romania and took over Bessarabia and Bukovina. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also, Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia became part of the Soviet Union. We also knew about Dunkirk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later on. I believe it was 1940, where the British and the French had to evacuate. We knew-- I knew about Vichy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- well, how did you feel, knowing all the things?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I feel? Well, we knew that we were alive. We felt terrible about the German army being able to blitzkrieg everybody, you know. Czechoslovakia was the first one, actually. How could you feel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the Germans were persecuting the Jews in the countries they took over?

speaker: interviewee
text: You heard. We knew what the cousin-- Mother's cousin told us. There were stories going around. But it was hard, you know. There were no proofs or anything substantial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe as a Jew in Kolomyia?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that particular time, yes. Between 1939 and '41, I felt safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know when Germany attacked Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they attacked us? Yes, positively.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Germany ever enter Kolomyia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Not-- I wasn't there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were gone by that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time I was already in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Tell me about going into the army. How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first let me tell you there was bombing again on the 21st of June, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, 1941. I beg your pardon. I skipped four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] The bombing was a daily a occurrence, twice a day, three times a day. Not the center of the city, mind you, but the roads like the railroad and so on outside the city. The-- we knew there's a war on. The Soviets were sending out draft notices to all the eligible men, 18 and older. Most of them were hiding. They did not want to join the Soviet army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are these the Jewish men or men in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men in-- I would say most Ukrainians-- Gentiles and Jews. They didn't want to go who knows where. And many of them didn't believe that the Germans would kill any civilian population.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was walking in the center of the city. It was like two days into the war, two or three days into the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was the same height like I'm right now. Much younger, I should say, and they had military police checking all the passports, identifications of all the men that they could spot in the-- in-- in the street to see how come you're not the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you received a notice. The notice came over the radio. All men report for military duty. And as nobody showed up, they want to know why. [LAUGHS] So they caught me, and I was only 16. I was 16 May 26, and this was June 21, 23, 24. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they brought me into the-- to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you protest that you're [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I protested that I was too young. I even showed them my passport that I had. It didn't help. You're old enough to fight for your country. They were desperate to get men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to tell your family what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was held in the barracks for two days, given a uniform, given a gun, and by that time, I was able-- I didn't come home. My mother realized what was happening. She came looking for me and she found me on the other side of the fence, which we couldn't go out. And she went to see the commander, and he says, we-- he's now a soldier. That's it. You can't. She went away crying, and that was the end, the last time I saw her. Two days later, we were marched out retreating.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have any training.

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever. They gave us a gun. They did show me how to use the gun. A rifle. One bullet each time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of unit were you assigned to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an infantry unit. That's it. And as we were retreating by horse and buggy, by walking, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the strafing by the airplanes were killing hundreds of people-- soldiers and civilians. We were retreating to the Russian border. Actually, we were part of the Soviet Union too. The old Soviet border. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody scattered, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even the military. Everybody was on their own. There was no discipline. Officers were killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a soldier, but I had no idea where I was going or what I was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people run--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just following the crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some ran away, yes. As I was walking, I bumped into a horse and wagon which was driven by somebody I knew in my hometown, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with his son that I knew from Kolomyja. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I hopped on the wagon, and we came across the old border. And we stopped near a place where I wanted to buy something to-- to get something to eat. I haven't eaten in a day and a half. As I went in and I came out, the horse and wagon were gone. The guy drove away without me. So I walked for about two hours. I came to a railroad station. In my uniform with my gun, I hopped on the train. There were hundreds and hundreds of soldiers from different units that hopped on the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were on the train for overnight, and in the morning, we wound up in Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- on-- on-- in the army, when you-- when you were taken into the army, did you find many other Jews in the army too that had been dragged in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were others.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you treated--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- well-- was I treated-- there was-- you didn't associate with too many people too long. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Right at the beginning of the war, I'm talking about. They were making remarks, yeah, [NON-ENGLISH] this and [NON-ENGLISH] that, you know. Jew this and Jew that, some of them. Some of them were kidding and saying a Jew shoots with a crooked rifle from around the corner. You must have heard that expression somewhere. They did make remarks. But they didn't kill you. You were part of the whole-- you were hungry. Everybody was hungry. You were miserable. Everybody was miserable. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you associate with other Jews in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You mean later in the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would-- you would be with the other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in different platoons, let's say, not in the same company. So when we had a little spare time, we used to get together and talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only Jew in your company?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say-- No, one of the officers was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could be Jewish and still become an officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, no two ways about it. Oh yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was promoted to sergeant. On the field, the sergeant was killed and apparently I was the only one who knew something more than the rest of them-- how to read and write, how to speak on the telephone, how to do this, how to do that, you know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about Kiev. You-- you retreated to Kiev.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kiev, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Kiev. What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the city was in turmoil. I came off the train. They had some people who told us where to go. They-- we had-- they had nowhere to put us to sleep. It was still summertime, mind you. It was June, the end of June the beginning of July, and they put us up to sleep in a park, a city park. We-- I had my coat, a roll like. I just put it down, and I slept in the park.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to contact the Jewish community of Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Jews of Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? Well, they knew what was happening. The war was on. The military police was rounding up all the stragglers. They picked me up again at the park, you know, where I was sleeping. They brought me into-- I explained to them what happened. They put me in a different unit, a completely different unit, all Russians. At least before there were a few people from my hometown. Here I was all by myself, completely different. And they sent us to the front. The front by that time was just outside Kiev. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't last there long. Order was retreat again. So we retreated as fast as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you retreat?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I walked. Then I hopped again on the wagon with horses. Then I was able to get on the truck, an old Russian truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: With other soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: With other soldiers. And we retreated and we wound up all way down to Kharkiv.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Kharkiv?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it's C-H-A-R-K-O-V, Kharkiv.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about Kharkiv.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I wasn't there too long either. [LAUGHS] They picked us up, and they started new units again. Because once you retreated you were on your own as far as you could go. Many of them were captured. You know. Many of them were killed, captured. I was one of the lucky few. I got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start from scratch again. They put you in-- in this unit, which was also-- what you call it? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't think of the word. Infantry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Infantry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Infantry. I couldn't think of the word. Anyway, it was still infantry, and they organized divisions. They were bringing in replacements, and-- you know, from Siberia. They were bringing in thousands of men, and they-- they were integrating the old ones and the new ones and all that stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept on switching from unit to unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: From unit to unit. Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were retreating and going through all of these different communities, did you ever have a chance to see any Jewish community or Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not until I got to the city of Kuibyshev, which was Samara--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the Volga River in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for two years you're retreating.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was retreating, retreating. I met a Jew here and a Jew there, but I was never with a Jewish community so to speak. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. From your experience between 1941 and 1943, how were the Jews in the military treated by the military? I would say the same as everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just soldiers. No. There were anti-Semites. It depends which part of the country some people came from. If you were a Russian peasant who came from kolkhoz, or came from Siberia--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kolkhoz is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is a-- in Hebrew a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a collective.

speaker: interviewee
text: Collective farm. Yes. If you came from-- from a kibbutz or a collective farm, you hardly-- I don't think you ever met a Jew. They had something, some preconceived notions, what their priest used to tell them a Jew was, like a black guy with horns on his head. But they really never met a Jew so they never knew what a Jew was. But when you came from a big city and-- you know, where anti-Semitism had a hold or something like that, then-- then there were anti-Semites, yes. Yes. If you ran across an officer who had a higher-- some education, they were anti-Semitic, some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had their upbringing, I guess, certain preconceived notions about what a Jew was or what he wasn't. You couldn't tell what certain people were brought up like. The church instilled it in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go to Kuibishev.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're at Kuibishev. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, when I was in that units between Kharkiv and Kuibishev, I was wounded, so I was sent to the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the hospital, like I said, they were-- I was like in any wounded soldier. They treated me well and so on. But instead of sending me back to the unit, they sent me to a factory where they were manufacturing airplanes. Because they desperately needed the parts to build the airplanes for the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked there for six months, 12-hour shifts-- as a soldier mind you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you getting paid for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Army pay, whatever that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did they get you money? How do they pay you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rubles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to a paymaster? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the army paid. Yeah, we had a paymaster in the army. And the food was hardly any. We also had in the same factory where we were working the 12-hour shifts, prisoners, Russian political prisoners. Criminals they put them to work. And they had worse than we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of these prisoners Jewish that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. I don't know. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they weren't Jews that had been rounded up to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rounded up, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There might have been some of them. I really couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they weren't there because they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't say that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, well, so they-- the food was hardly anything, a little soup. There was nothing in it, mostly water. And they gave us 200 grams of black bread, which was mostly sawdust. They mixed it with sawdust to stretch it. The prisoners, on the other hand, were getting nothing, practically. They were starving in the cold outside. They used them just like a concentration camp, I would say. When we threw away the garbage, the soldiers, they used to fight for the garbage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do anything to help any of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't. You really couldn't do anything. It was like a-- animals. We-- this-- the-- their guards, you couldn't even come close to them. They had the [INAUDIBLE] guards, you know, the secret or whatever police, which was, you know, there all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was nothing that you could do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We weren't so free either. We were soldiers, and we had a special card with our picture on it and a number where we could get in and out. That's all we could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near the factory, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And only men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any social life? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No social life, no nothing. I was able to go out. When-- when I got the end of the month, I got a couple of rubles, and I was hungry. I used to go out to the market. The markets still had Russian peasant women selling from their farms whatever. You know, they had their own plots, because most farms were collective farms, so they had their own plots.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they used to make [NON-ENGLISH], they called it, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], but they were baked with potato or whatever and they were very expensive. But whenever it was, I-- I blew my whole month's salary on a couple of these things so I could eat something, [LAUGHS] you know, and survive. And you were there for six months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to-- five-- five and a half months, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the crops were coming in on the kohlkoz, which is the collective farm. They had no men to take them off, so the men were taken off the factories and sent there. That was September sometimes. And we were sent to take off the food from the collective farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And still getting paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Still getting paid by the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still the same thing. Paid. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but instead of staying in barracks, we stayed individually with peasant families in their homes. Each soldier or two soldiers were assigned to one family. And besides working so many hours taking off the wheat, they were afraid that the Germans were going to break through, so they put us for hours digging trenches.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the army retreats, they'll have trenches already prepared for the army that would back up. I understand. So how long we are at the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two and a half months.

speaker: interviewer
text: That puts you into the winter now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And this is winter of '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I would say it was the winter-- the end of '42 the beginning of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And where did you go after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, I'm trying to remember. After this, I was put into this outfit, the [? mortar ?] division. Now this was a real organized unit. I was already 18, and we had formal training in this unit. We had how to handle the armaments. I was given a sub-machine gun with 74 round bullets, you know, automatic. We, for three, four months, we got up every-- early in the morning training-- running, jumping all over things, you know, like obstacles. Lectures, what the fascists were doing to the country, how they were killing women and children. They instilled a hate in us for the Germans, the Nazis, with no mercy. They were killing our families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any opportunity or any way of contacting your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- it was in the Nazi territory. It was an occupied territory. I was in-- near Kuibishev, near [INAUDIBLE]. It was completely different. This was Soviet-dominated area. And that was the German-dominated. Between us was the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: You retreated all the way back to Stalingrad, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What happened is after the training, they put us on a boat on the Volga River. They brought in a lot of smaller boats, and we were shipped down the Volga River to a certain point where we landed on the Western side of the Volga River. And we were part of the units that surrounded [INAUDIBLE]. You know, circle. They tried to break out. They couldn't. We're going to pause here, Jack, to change the tape again.
